ingredients.summary: Live beneficial microorganisms; extensive clinical evidence for gut health, immunity, and more.
ingredients.fullDescription: Probiotics are live bacteria and yeasts that, when consumed in adequate amounts, confer health benefits. The most studied strains include Lactobacillus acidophilus, L. rhamnosus GG, Bifidobacterium longum, and Saccharomyces boulardii. A 2019 Cochrane review found probiotics reduce antibiotic-associated diarrhea by 60%. Meta-analyses support their use for IBS symptom reduction, prevention of C. difficile infection, and modulation of immune responses. Strain-specificity matters significantly — different strains have different evidence bases.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: regulated as dietary supplements or food ingredients (not drugs); EFSA: specific health claims under review
ingredients.safeLimit: No established limit; typical doses 10^9-10^10 CFU/day in studies
ingredients.concerns: ["Immunocompromised individuals should consult physician before use", "Strain-specific benefits \u2014 not all probiotic products are equivalent"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Cochrane 2019: 60% reduction in antibiotic-associated diarrhea", "Reduces IBS symptoms in multiple meta-analyses", "Modulates immune function", "Helps prevent C. difficile infection", "Supports gut microbiome diversity"]

ingredients.summary: Table salt; essential mineral but excess intake causes hypertension and cardiovascular disease.
ingredients.fullDescription: Sodium chloride (table salt) is an essential mineral required for fluid balance, nerve conduction, and muscle function. However, the average American consumes approximately 3,400mg of sodium daily, far exceeding the AHA-recommended 2,300mg limit (1,500mg for high-risk individuals). High sodium intake is causally linked to hypertension, which is a primary risk factor for stroke and heart disease (WHO estimates 1.89 million deaths annually attributable to high sodium intake).
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS; WHO recommends <2000mg sodium/day; AHA recommends <2300mg/day

ingredients.benefits: ["Essential mineral for fluid balance and nerve function", "Natural preservative and flavor enhancer"]

ingredients.summary: Refined sucrose; excess consumption linked to obesity, type 2 diabetes, and dental caries.
ingredients.fullDescription: Sucrose (table sugar) is a disaccharide of glucose and fructose extracted from sugarcane or sugar beets. While a natural substance, the WHO recommends limiting free sugars to <10% of total energy intake (<5% for additional health benefit) due to strong evidence linking excess consumption to dental caries, obesity, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. The average American consumes roughly 77g of added sugar per day — more than triple the AHA recommendation for women.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS; WHO: free sugars <10% of energy; AHA: added sugar <25g/day women, <36g/day men
ingredients.safeLimit: WHO: <10% of total energy from free sugars; AHA: <25g added sugar/day for women, <36g for men
ingredients.concerns: ["Primary cause of dental caries \u2014 WHO: free sugars <10% of energy to reduce caries risk", "Associated with obesity and metabolic syndrome (WHO Global Action Plan on NCDs)", "WHO: free sugars contribute to non-communicable disease burden globally", "Average US intake of added sugars 3x exceeds AHA recommendations"]

ingredients.summary: Simple sugar and primary energy source; rapidly raises blood glucose when consumed as additive.
ingredients.fullDescription: Glucose (dextrose) is the primary fuel for the brain and muscles and is the body's baseline blood sugar. As a food additive derived from corn starch hydrolysis, it raises blood glucose rapidly (GI ~100 — the reference standard). While essential for life, consuming concentrated glucose as a food additive contributes to rapid glycemic spikes, promoting insulin resistance over time with frequent exposure. It is commonly added to processed foods for sweetness and browning.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS; WHO: included in 'free sugars' recommendation
ingredients.safeLimit: Covered by WHO free sugars recommendation (<10% of energy)

ingredients.benefits: ["Primary cellular fuel", "Rapid energy source for athletes", "Essential bodily function \u2014 not inherently harmful in normal amounts"]

ingredients.summary: Fruit sugar metabolized by liver; excess intake promotes fatty liver and metabolic dysfunction.
ingredients.fullDescription: Fructose is a monosaccharide that differs from glucose in its hepatic metabolism — virtually all fructose is processed by the liver where it bypasses insulin regulation and can be converted to fat (de novo lipogenesis) and uric acid. Added fructose (in HFCS, table sugar, and as a standalone additive) at high doses is associated with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, hyperuricemia, insulin resistance, and hypertriglyceridemia. Whole fruit intake, despite containing fructose, is not associated with these outcomes due to fiber co-content.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS; WHO: included in free sugars recommendation
ingredients.safeLimit: WHO: covered by free sugars limit; experts recommend <25g/day added fructose

ingredients.benefits: ["Naturally present in fruits (low concern with whole fruit)", "Low glycemic index \u2014 does not raise blood glucose directly"]

ingredients.summary: Starch thickener from corn; neutral impact at typical food use levels.
ingredients.fullDescription: Corn starch is a polysaccharide extracted from corn kernels, used primarily as a thickening agent in sauces, gravies, and baked goods. As a refined starch, it has a high glycemic index (~85) and minimal nutritional value beyond carbohydrate energy. At typical use levels as a thickener, the quantity consumed is too small to significantly impact glycemic response. The FDA considers it GRAS.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS; EU: permitted food ingredient
ingredients.concerns: ["High glycemic index (~85) \u2014 relevant if consumed in large quantities", "Minimal nutritional value"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Effective thickener at small quantities", "Gluten-free alternative to wheat flour thickeners"]

ingredients.summary: Chemically or physically modified starch; widely used but processing agents raise some concerns.
ingredients.fullDescription: Modified corn starch is corn starch treated with acids, enzymes, heat, or chemical agents (such as phosphates or acetates) to alter its functional properties — improving stability, thickening, or freeze-thaw resistance. The modification process can introduce reagent residues. Phosphate-modified starches (E1410–E1414) may contribute to phosphate load, which is associated with cardiovascular and kidney risks at high intake. EFSA has reviewed individual modified starches and considers them acceptable at current use levels.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS; EU: E1400-series with specific ADIs depending on modification type; EFSA: reviewed individually
ingredients.safeLimit: Varies by modification type; phosphate-modified forms have EU maximum use levels
ingredients.concerns: ["Chemical modification agents may leave residues \u2014 EFSA reviews each E-number individually", "Phosphate-modified forms (E1410-E1414) contribute to excess dietary phosphate \u2014 EFSA upper level 70 mg/kg bw/day", "FDA GRAS but EFSA sets maximum use levels for phosphate-modified starches (Regulation EC No 1333/2008)"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Superior freeze-thaw stability vs native starch", "Improved texture and shelf life in processed foods"]

ingredients.summary: Cassava-derived starch; gluten-free thickener with neutral health profile.
ingredients.fullDescription: Tapioca starch (tapioca flour) is extracted from the cassava root (Manihot esculenta). It is widely used as a thickener and texturizer and is naturally gluten-free, making it popular in gluten-free baking. Like other refined starches, it has a high glycemic index and minimal nutritional value. At typical food use levels as a thickener, it does not significantly affect health. No safety concerns have been identified by FDA or EFSA.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS; EU: permitted food ingredient
ingredients.concerns: ["High glycemic index \u2014 similar to white rice", "Minimal nutritional value"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Gluten-free \u2014 safe for celiac disease", "Neutral flavor", "Effective binding and thickening in gluten-free baking"]

ingredients.summary: Highly processed starch derivative; extremely high glycemic index, promotes gut dysbiosis.
ingredients.fullDescription: Maltodextrin is a polysaccharide produced by partial hydrolysis of starch (typically corn, wheat, or potato). Despite being technically a 'complex carbohydrate,' it has a glycemic index of 85–105 — higher than table sugar. Research has shown maltodextrin promotes growth of Escherichia coli strains associated with Crohn's disease and disrupts gut mucosal biofilm (NIH study, PLoS ONE 2012). It is widely used in protein powders, diet foods, and fat replacers.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS; EU: permitted food ingredient; no ADI established
ingredients.safeLimit: No ADI established; covered by general sugar/starch guidance
ingredients.concerns: ["Glycemic index 85-105 \u2014 higher than table sugar (EFSA carbohydrate DRI)", "Promotes E. coli biofilm associated with Crohn's disease (PLoS ONE 2012, PubMed PMID:22928058)", "Disrupts gut mucosal barrier (EWG: moderate concern for gut health impacts)", "WHO: included in free sugars context \u2014 contributes to excess carbohydrate load"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Cost-effective bulking and texturing agent", "Tasteless \u2014 does not alter food flavor"]

ingredients.summary: Catch-all FDA term for flavor compounds from natural sources; composition is proprietary.
ingredients.fullDescription: The FDA defines 'natural flavors' as substances derived from natural sources (plant or animal) primarily for their flavoring function. The term can encompass hundreds of individual chemicals; the specific composition is considered a trade secret and need not be disclosed. This opacity makes it difficult for consumers with allergies or dietary restrictions to assess safety. Natural flavors may contain solvents, preservatives, and emulsifiers (up to 80% of the flavor preparation may be non-flavor ingredients). They are generally considered safe by regulators.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA 21 CFR 101.22: defined category; EU: must specify natural or artificial origin
ingredients.safeLimit: No ADI established; safety evaluated on a per-compound basis
ingredients.concerns: ["Composition not fully disclosed \u2014 allergen or dietary restriction concerns", "May contain solvents and carrier ingredients not disclosed", "Animal-derived natural flavors may not be vegan/vegetarian"]

ingredients.summary: Synthetically produced flavor chemicals; generally safe individually but composition is opaque.
ingredients.fullDescription: Artificial flavors are synthetic chemical compounds that mimic natural flavors. The FDA requires only the collective term 'artificial flavors' on labels, not disclosure of individual compounds. While individual flavor chemicals are evaluated for safety by the Flavor and Extract Manufacturers Association (FEMA) before use, the cumulative effect of consuming many synthetic flavor compounds across multiple products is not well-studied. Some individuals report sensitivity reactions to specific artificial flavor chemicals such as vanillin or benzaldehyde.

ingredients.safeLimit: Varies by specific compound; FEMA GRAS evaluated individually
ingredients.concerns: ["Composition not disclosed \u2014 FDA 21 CFR 101.22 requires only collective term, not individual compounds", "Cumulative exposure to multiple synthetic flavor chemicals \u2014 FDA evaluates individually, not in combination", "EWG: transparency concern \u2014 hundreds of possible compounds hidden under one label term"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Lower cost than natural flavors", "More stable flavor profile than natural extracts"]

ingredients.summary: Essential for all life; listed as an ingredient in many processed foods.
ingredients.fullDescription: Water is an essential component of all living organisms and is required for all biochemical processes. When listed as a food ingredient, it typically functions as a carrier, solvent for other ingredients, or moisture contributor to texture. Adequate hydration (about 2–3L total water intake per day for adults from all sources) is associated with improved kidney function, cognitive performance, and general health. As an ingredient, it carries no health concerns.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: drinking water regulated under Safe Drinking Water Act; not a food additive per se
ingredients.safeLimit: No upper limit under normal conditions (rare hyponatremia from extreme overhydration)
ingredients.concerns: ["None at normal consumption levels"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Essential for life and all metabolic processes", "Zero calories", "Promotes kidney health and hydration", "Improves cognitive performance when adequate hydration maintained"]

ingredients.summary: Vitamin C; essential antioxidant, immune support, and collagen synthesis cofactor.
ingredients.fullDescription: Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) is a water-soluble essential vitamin and powerful antioxidant. It is required for collagen biosynthesis, immune function, and iron absorption. The human body cannot synthesize vitamin C (unlike most mammals), making dietary intake essential. Deficiency causes scurvy. When added to foods, it also serves as an antioxidant preservative. Multiple meta-analyses support its role in reducing the severity and duration of the common cold and its cardiovascular benefits.

ingredients.summary: Vitamin E; fat-soluble antioxidant essential for immune function and skin health.
ingredients.fullDescription: Tocopherols are a family of fat-soluble compounds comprising Vitamin E, with alpha-tocopherol being the most biologically active form. As a food additive, tocopherols serve as antioxidants preventing fat oxidation and rancidity. As an essential vitamin, Vitamin E protects cell membranes from oxidative damage, supports immune function, and has anti-inflammatory properties. It is found naturally in nuts, seeds, and vegetable oils. The FDA and EFSA consider natural tocopherols safe at food use levels.

ingredients.summary: Vitamin B2; essential for energy metabolism and cellular function, also used as yellow food color.
ingredients.fullDescription: Riboflavin (Vitamin B2) is a water-soluble B vitamin essential for the metabolism of carbohydrates, fats, and proteins as a cofactor in FAD and FMN coenzymes. It is also used as a natural yellow food colorant. Deficiency causes ariboflavinosis (angular stomatitis, glossitis, skin rashes). The FDA classifies it as GRAS. Because it is water-soluble, excess amounts are excreted by the kidneys and toxicity is not reported from food sources.

ingredients.concerns: ["None at food or typical supplemental doses \u2014 water-soluble, excreted"]

ingredients.summary: Vitamin B3; essential for energy metabolism; therapeutic doses raise HDL and lower LDL.
ingredients.fullDescription: Niacin (Vitamin B3, nicotinic acid) is a water-soluble vitamin essential for NAD and NADP coenzyme function in energy metabolism, DNA repair, and cell signaling. At therapeutic doses (1–3g/day), prescription niacin is FDA-approved for treating dyslipidemia — raising HDL, lowering LDL and triglycerides. At supplemental doses above 35mg, it can cause the 'niacin flush' (skin reddening). Deficiency causes pellagra. Corn-based diets without nixtamalization are deficient in available niacin.

ingredients.summary: Synthetic form of folate (B9); critical for neural tube development, mandatory enrichment in US.
ingredients.fullDescription: Folic acid is the synthetic oxidized form of folate (Vitamin B9), essential for DNA synthesis, cell division, and amino acid metabolism. The US FDA mandates folic acid enrichment of grain products since 1998, after evidence showed it prevents neural tube defects (NTDs) in pregnancy — the CDC estimates enrichment prevents 1,300 NTDs annually. Adequate folate also reduces homocysteine levels associated with cardiovascular disease. Unmetabolized folic acid from excessive supplementation may mask B12 deficiency in older adults.

ingredients.concerns: ["High doses may mask vitamin B12 deficiency anemia (leading to delayed diagnosis)", "Unmetabolized folic acid accumulation in blood from very high supplemental doses"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Prevents neural tube defects \u2014 CDC: prevents 1300 NTDs/year in US", "Essential for DNA synthesis and cell division", "Reduces homocysteine \u2014 cardiovascular protection", "Critical in pregnancy and for rapidly dividing cells"]

ingredients.summary: Vitamin B1; essential for carbohydrate metabolism and nerve function.
ingredients.fullDescription: Thiamine (Vitamin B1) is a water-soluble vitamin that serves as a cofactor for enzymes involved in carbohydrate metabolism and branched-chain amino acid catabolism. Deficiency causes beriberi (peripheral neuropathy, cardiovascular dysfunction) and Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome in alcoholism. Because it is heat-labile and water-soluble, food processing depletes thiamine, necessitating enrichment. The FDA mandates its addition to enriched grain products. No known toxicity from food or typical supplement sources.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS; mandatory enrichment of grains; RDA: 1.1-1.2 mg/day; no UL established
ingredients.concerns: ["None at food or supplemental levels \u2014 water-soluble and excreted"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Essential for carbohydrate energy metabolism", "Nerve function and conduction", "Prevents beriberi and Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome", "No toxicity \u2014 excreted in urine"]

ingredients.summary: Essential trace mineral; immune function, wound healing, and enzyme cofactor.
ingredients.fullDescription: Zinc is an essential trace mineral required by over 300 enzymes involved in metabolism, growth, immune function, and DNA synthesis. It plays a critical role in wound healing, taste perception, and immune response. Cochrane systematic review evidence supports zinc supplementation for reducing duration and severity of the common cold. Deficiency impairs immune function and causes growth retardation. The tolerable upper intake level is 40mg/day to avoid copper deficiency from competitive absorption inhibition.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS as food supplement; RDA: 8-11 mg/day; UL: 40 mg/day
ingredients.safeLimit: RDA 8-11 mg/day; UL 40 mg/day (inhibits copper absorption above this)
ingredients.concerns: ["Excess supplementation (>40mg/day) inhibits copper absorption causing deficiency", "Very high doses cause GI distress", "Zinc nasal sprays associated with anosmia \u2014 different route of concern"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Cochrane: reduces cold duration and severity", "Essential for immune system function", "Required for wound healing", "Cofactor for 300+ enzymes", "Important for taste and smell"]

ingredients.summary: Essential mineral for oxygen transport and energy metabolism; important to balance intake carefully.
ingredients.fullDescription: Iron is an essential mineral required for hemoglobin synthesis, myoglobin, and numerous enzyme systems. Iron-deficiency anemia affects approximately 1.6 billion people globally (WHO), making iron fortification an important public health strategy. However, excess iron supplementation in iron-replete individuals can cause oxidative damage, as iron catalyzes the Fenton reaction generating hydroxyl radicals. Heme iron (from meat) is more bioavailable than non-heme iron (from plants). Vitamin C enhances non-heme iron absorption.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: RDA 8-18mg/day; UL 45mg/day; mandatory enrichment of many grain products
ingredients.safeLimit: RDA 8-18 mg/day; UL 45 mg/day; treatment doses under medical supervision
ingredients.concerns: ["Excess iron supplementation causes oxidative damage via Fenton reaction", "GI side effects common with supplementation (constipation, nausea)", "Iron toxicity \u2014 leading cause of pediatric poisoning from supplement overdose"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Essential for oxygen transport via hemoglobin", "Required for energy metabolism and enzyme function", "WHO: iron deficiency anemia affects 1.6 billion people \u2014 fortification critical", "Vitamin C enhances absorption"]

ingredients.summary: Most abundant mineral in the body; essential for bone health, muscle function, and nerve signaling.
ingredients.fullDescription: Calcium is the most abundant mineral in the human body, with 99% stored in bones and teeth. It is essential for bone mineralization, muscle contraction (including cardiac muscle), nerve signal transmission, and blood clotting. Adequate calcium intake throughout life reduces osteoporosis risk. However, excessive supplemental calcium (>2500mg/day) may increase risk of kidney stones and has been associated with cardiovascular calcification in some studies. Food sources are preferred over high-dose supplements.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS; RDA: 1000-1200 mg/day; UL: 2500 mg/day
ingredients.concerns: ["Very high supplemental doses (>2500mg/day) linked to kidney stones", "Some meta-analyses: high supplemental calcium associated with increased cardiovascular risk"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Essential for bone and teeth mineralization", "Prevents osteoporosis with adequate lifetime intake", "Required for muscle contraction and nerve signaling", "Supports blood clotting"]

ingredients.summary: Essential mineral cofactor for 300+ enzymes; most people fall short of adequate intake.
ingredients.fullDescription: Magnesium is a cofactor in over 300 enzymatic reactions including energy production (ATP synthesis), protein synthesis, muscle and nerve function, blood glucose regulation, and blood pressure regulation. Approximately 50% of Americans consume less than the recommended daily amount. A meta-analysis in BMJ Open (2016) found higher magnesium intake associated with reduced risk of cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and all-cause mortality. Magnesium from food sources is safe; supplemental forms can cause diarrhea above 350mg/day from supplements.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA GRAS; RDA: 310-420 mg/day; supplemental UL: 350 mg/day
ingredients.safeLimit: RDA 310-420 mg/day; supplemental UL 350 mg/day (above this: osmotic diarrhea)
ingredients.concerns: ["Supplemental doses above 350mg/day cause osmotic diarrhea", "Very high IV doses (medical settings): cardiac effects"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Cofactor for 300+ enzymes including ATP synthesis", "BMJ Open 2016: higher intake associated with reduced CVD, T2D, and all-cause mortality", "Supports blood pressure regulation", "Muscle and nerve function", "Blood glucose regulation"]

ingredients.summary: Wheat protein complex causing celiac disease and NCGS in susceptible individuals.
ingredients.fullDescription: Gluten is a family of proteins (gliadins and glutenins) found in wheat, rye, and barley. In individuals with celiac disease (1% of the population), gluten triggers an autoimmune response damaging the small intestinal villi, causing malabsorption, diarrhea, and long-term complications including anemia, osteoporosis, and increased cancer risk. Non-celiac gluten sensitivity (NCGS) affects an estimated 6% of the population with symptoms that resolve on a gluten-free diet. The FDA requires mandatory labeling of wheat as an allergen.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: wheat is a mandatory Top-9 allergen; EU: mandatory allergen declaration; Codex standard for gluten-free (<20 ppm)
ingredients.safeLimit: Celiac disease: strict gluten-free diet required (<20 ppm for labeled gluten-free foods)
ingredients.concerns: ["Causes autoimmune celiac disease in ~1% of population \u2014 NIH/NIDDK and Celiac Disease Foundation", "Non-celiac gluten sensitivity (NCGS) affects estimated 6% of population (WHO/FAO food allergy guidance)", "FDA mandatory allergen labeling: wheat is Top-9 allergen (FALCPA 2004)"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Provides structural properties in baking for the general population without gluten sensitivity"]

ingredients.summary: Common major food allergen; soy allergy is among top 9 FDA-recognized allergens.
ingredients.fullDescription: Soy protein is derived from soybeans (Glycine max) and is a major food allergen, classified as one of the FDA's Top-9 allergens requiring mandatory labeling since 2004. Soy allergy affects approximately 0.4% of children and 0.3% of adults. Soy proteins contain multiple allergenic proteins including Gly m 5 and Gly m 6. Most individuals with soy allergy can tolerate highly refined soybean oil and soy lecithin, but whole soy protein is problematic. Additionally, soy contains phytoestrogens (isoflavones) whose effects on human hormonal health remain debated.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: mandatory Top-9 allergen labeling; EU: mandatory allergen declaration; FALCPA 2004
ingredients.safeLimit: Individuals with soy allergy: complete avoidance required
ingredients.concerns: ["Major food allergen \u2014 FDA mandatory Top-9 allergen labeling required (FALCPA 2004)", "Anaphylaxis risk in sensitized individuals (EFSA food allergy panel)", "Phytoestrogen (isoflavone) content \u2014 hormonal effects debated (EFSA soy isoflavones review 2015)"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Complete protein containing all essential amino acids", "Heart-healthy fat profile", "Source of isoflavones with potential cardiovascular benefits in some studies"]

ingredients.summary: Primary milk protein; major allergen requiring mandatory labeling; distinct from lactose intolerance.
ingredients.fullDescription: Casein comprises approximately 80% of bovine milk protein and is the primary allergen responsible for milk allergy, which is distinct from lactose intolerance. Milk allergy affects about 2–3% of infants and 0.5% of adults and can cause urticaria, angioedema, and anaphylaxis. Casein is heat-stable and survives processing, remaining allergenic in all dairy products. The FDA mandates dairy as a Top-9 allergen. Casein is also used as an ingredient in some non-dairy products, making label reading essential for allergic individuals.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: dairy is a mandatory Top-9 allergen; EU: mandatory allergen declaration; FALCPA 2004
ingredients.concerns: ["IgE-mediated anaphylaxis in milk-allergic individuals \u2014 FDA mandatory Top-9 allergen (FALCPA 2004)", "Hidden in non-dairy products \u2014 FDA requires 'contains milk' declaration on all dairy-containing foods", "EFSA: milk allergy affects 0.5% of adults; mandatory EU allergen declaration"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Slow-digesting protein \u2014 sustained amino acid release", "Complete protein with all essential amino acids", "Calcium source in dairy context"]

ingredients.summary: Complete protein source and major food allergen; egg allergy is among top 9 allergens.
ingredients.fullDescription: Egg allergy is the second most common food allergy in children (after peanut), affecting approximately 1.3% of children globally. The primary allergens are ovomucoid, ovalbumin, and lysozyme in egg white; egg yolk allergens are less common. Most children outgrow egg allergy by adolescence. The FDA mandates egg as a Top-9 allergen. Egg is an excellent source of complete protein, choline, and fat-soluble vitamins for non-allergic individuals.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: mandatory Top-9 allergen labeling; EU: mandatory allergen declaration; FALCPA 2004
ingredients.safeLimit: Egg-allergic individuals: avoidance required; baked forms may be tolerated (consult allergist)
ingredients.concerns: ["Second most common food allergy in children", "Anaphylaxis risk in sensitized individuals", "Mandatory allergen labeling required"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Complete protein with all essential amino acids", "Rich in choline \u2014 essential for brain function", "Source of fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K", "Lutein and zeaxanthin for eye health"]

ingredients.summary: Legume, not a nut; one of the most common and severe food allergens worldwide.
ingredients.fullDescription: Peanut (Arachis hypogaea) allergy is one of the most common and severe food allergies, affecting approximately 1–2% of the general population. Unlike many childhood food allergies, peanut allergy is rarely outgrown (80% lifelong). It is a leading cause of fatal and near-fatal anaphylaxis. The primary allergens are Ara h 1, 2, and 3. The FDA mandates peanuts as a Top-9 allergen. For non-allergic individuals, peanuts are nutritionally beneficial as a good source of protein, healthy fats, and niacin.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: mandatory Top-9 allergen labeling; EU: mandatory allergen declaration; primary cause of food allergy fatalities
ingredients.safeLimit: Peanut-allergic individuals: strict avoidance required; even trace amounts can cause anaphylaxis
ingredients.concerns: ["Leading cause of fatal food anaphylaxis in the US \u2014 FDA mandatory Top-9 allergen (FALCPA 2004)", "80% of peanut allergy is lifelong \u2014 NIH NIAID food allergy guidelines", "EFSA: peanut allergy among most severe, mandatory EU allergen declaration"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Excellent protein source for non-allergic individuals", "Rich in monounsaturated fats \u2014 cardiovascular benefits", "Source of niacin, magnesium, and resveratrol"]

ingredients.summary: Includes almonds, walnuts, cashews, and others; major allergen with lifelong persistence.
ingredients.fullDescription: Tree nut allergy encompasses reactions to almonds, walnuts, cashews, pistachios, pecans, Brazil nuts, hazelnuts, macadamia nuts, and others. It affects approximately 0.5–1% of the general population. Like peanut allergy, tree nut allergy is often lifelong (9% resolution rate) and can cause severe anaphylaxis. Cross-reactivity between different tree nuts is common but not universal. The FDA mandates tree nuts as a Top-9 allergen with individual nut types specified. For non-allergic individuals, tree nuts are associated with cardiovascular health benefits.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: mandatory Top-9 allergen labeling (each nut specified); EU: mandatory allergen declaration; FALCPA 2004
ingredients.safeLimit: Tree-nut-allergic individuals: avoidance of all tree nuts required unless individual allergy profiled
ingredients.concerns: ["Severe anaphylaxis risk \u2014 FDA mandatory Top-9 allergen (each nut specified) (FALCPA 2004)", "9% resolution rate \u2014 usually lifelong allergy (EFSA food allergy panel reports)", "WHO/EFSA: mandatory allergen declaration required; cross-contamination risk high"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Rich in heart-healthy monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats (for non-allergic)", "Source of vitamin E, magnesium, selenium", "Associated with reduced cardiovascular disease risk in large cohort studies"]

ingredients.summary: Fish allergy is a major allergen, often lifelong; distinct from shellfish allergy.
ingredients.fullDescription: Fish allergy affects approximately 1% of adults and is usually lifelong (only 5% outgrow it). The primary allergen in most fish is parvalbumin, a calcium-binding muscle protein. Fish allergy is distinct from shellfish allergy — an individual may be allergic to one and not the other. The FDA mandates fish as a Top-9 allergen with the specific fish type declared. Cross-reactivity between fish species is common but not universal. Fish with scales generally cross-react more than finfish vs. shellfish.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: mandatory Top-9 allergen labeling (specific fish named); EU: mandatory allergen declaration; FALCPA 2004
ingredients.safeLimit: Fish-allergic individuals: avoidance required for specific fish species; individual allergy profile needed
ingredients.concerns: ["Anaphylaxis risk in sensitized individuals", "Usually lifelong allergy (only 5% outgrow)", "Hidden in fish sauce, Worcestershire sauce, and Caesar dressing", "Mandatory allergen labeling required with specific fish named"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Excellent source of lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids for non-allergic individuals", "Rich in vitamin D and iodine"]

ingredients.summary: Shellfish allergy is the most common adult food allergy; often severe and lifelong.
ingredients.fullDescription: Shellfish allergy is the most prevalent adult food allergy, affecting approximately 2% of adults in the US. It includes crustaceans (shrimp, crab, lobster) and mollusks (oysters, clams, scallops). The primary allergen in crustaceans is tropomyosin, which is highly cross-reactive between shellfish species. Shellfish allergy is rarely outgrown and carries high risk of severe anaphylaxis. The FDA mandates crustacean shellfish as a Top-9 allergen (mollusks currently not mandatory but recommended to declare).
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: crustacean shellfish mandatory Top-9 allergen; EU: mandatory declaration for crustaceans and mollusks; FALCPA 2004
ingredients.safeLimit: Shellfish-allergic individuals: avoidance required; cross-reactivity between crustaceans is near-universal
ingredients.concerns: ["Most common adult food allergy (2% of US adults)", "Anaphylaxis risk \u2014 frequently severe", "Cross-contamination in seafood restaurants very common", "Near-universal cross-reactivity among crustaceans"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Lean protein and minerals (zinc, selenium, iodine) for non-allergic individuals", "Shellfish omega-3 content contributes to cardiovascular health"]

ingredients.summary: Sesame allergy recently added as 9th major allergen in US (FASTER Act 2023); can cause anaphylaxis.
ingredients.fullDescription: Sesame allergy affects approximately 0.23% of the US population and became the 9th mandatory allergen for labeling in the US effective January 1, 2023 under the FASTER Act. The primary sesame allergens are Ses i 1 through Ses i 7. Sesame allergy can cause anaphylaxis and is often lifelong. Unlike some common allergens, sesame is used in many diverse food products from Middle Eastern cuisine (tahini, hummus) to baked goods and Asian sauces, making avoidance challenging. There is limited cross-reactivity with other seeds.
ingredients.regulatoryStatus: FDA: mandatory Top-9 allergen labeling effective January 1, 2023 (FASTER Act); EU: mandatory allergen declaration
ingredients.concerns: ["FDA 9th major allergen since January 2023 (FASTER Act)", "Anaphylaxis risk", "Widely hidden in diverse cuisines and products", "Limited cross-reactivity with poppy and kiwi seeds in some individuals"]
ingredients.benefits: ["Rich in monounsaturated fats and antioxidant sesaminol for non-allergic individuals", "Good source of calcium and zinc"]
